definition: 32 GT/s per lane (~64 GB/s x16 bidirectional). The mainstream data-center IO standard for current-gen CPUs, GPUs, NICs, and NVMe; also the physical layer for CXL 1.1/2.0.
Doubles PCIe 5.0 to 64 GT/s using PAM4 signaling and FEC. Enables faster GPUs/NICs and is the basis for CXL 3.x — critical for AI networking bandwidth.
The next PCIe generation targeting 128 GT/s per lane, aimed at future AI, 800G+ networking, and CXL — spec finalized ahead of broad product availability.
An open industry standard for scale-up accelerator-to-accelerator interconnect (AMD, Broadcom, HPE, Intel, etc.) — the consortium's open alternative to NVIDIA's proprietary NVLink.
The Ultra Ethernet Consortium's effort to modernize Ethernet for AI/HPC scale-out — better congestion control, multipathing, and low latency — as an open alternative to InfiniBand.

Scale-up = tightly couple accelerators within a node/rack over a fast fabric (NVLink, UALink) so they act as one big GPU. Scale-out = connect many nodes over a network (Ethernet/InfiniBand). AI needs both tiers.
A low-latency, high-throughput HPC/AI network with RDMA and in-network compute. Dominant in large GPU training clusters; NVIDIA's Quantum line is the leading implementation.
An open standard for die-to-die interconnect, enabling chiplets from different vendors to be packaged together — foundational to the industry's move to disaggregated silicon.
Remote Direct Memory Access lets one server read/write another's memory bypassing the CPU/OS for very low latency. RoCE runs RDMA over Ethernet — key to AI fabrics on Ethernet.
